coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon dots for tartrazine sensing and mechanistic perception of their radical scavenging activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shaik, Md Abdus Salam
Samanta, Dipanjan
Shaw, Manisha
Mondal, Imran
Basu, Rajarshi
Pathak, Amita
coredata.dc:description: Blue fluorescent, biocompatible, nitrogen & sulphur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs), prepared via microwave-assisted pyrolysis of tartaric acid and thiourea, have been explored as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of the yellow-coloured food additive, tartrazine, in the aqueous medium through fluorescence quenching mechanism. The NSCDs (quantum yield ∼ 26%) exhibited static quenching in presence of tartrazine with the limit of detection (LOD) of 48 nM in the linear concentration range from 1 to 50 μM. In addition, NSCDs have also been investigated for their antioxidant activity by probing their ability to scavenge various reactive oxygen species (e.g., superoxide, peroxide, hydroxyl, and KMnO4) along with their total antioxidant capacity and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate (DPPH) free radicals premised on UV–Vis spectroscopy. From pH-dependent UV–Vis and EPR spectroscopy studies, the scavenging of DPPH free radicals by the NSCDs was affirmed to follow the hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism in an acidic medium while the proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) mechanism was predominant in alkaline medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NSCDs
Tartrazine
Sensing
Scavenging
DPPH
HAT
PCET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Plausible mechanism of scavenging activity by NSCD

The authors would like to acknowledge the Central Research facility and the Department of Chemistry, IIT Kharagpur India. Md A S Shaik would also like to acknowledge IIT Kharagpur , India for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) X-ray diffraction pattern of NSCDs of 2θ ranging from 5 to 80; (B) IR Spectrum of NSCDs; (C) HRTEM image and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (inset) of NSCDs.
(A) XPS full survey of NSCDs; high resolution spectra of (B) C 1 s; (C) N 1 s; (D) S 2p of NSCDs.
UV–Vis spectrum of NSCDs in the range of 200 to 500 nm wavelength.

(A) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of NSCDs in the range of 310 nm to 380 nm wavelength; (B) pH-dependent fluorescence spectra of NSCDs were recorded at 360 nm excitation wavelength from pH=3 to 11.

A) Fluorescence quenching spectra of NSCDs in presence of Tartrazine from 5 to 45 µL; B) Stern-Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching study of NSCDs in presence of tartrazine taken at 360 nm excitation wavelength; C) UV–Vis spectra of Tartrazine in presence of NSCDs; D) fluorescence lifetime decays of NSCDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Tartrazine.
Comparison of fluorescence intensities of NSCDs after the addition of different; (A) Metal ion (B) Other foreign substances of same concentration.
EPR spectra of DPPH were recorded after the addition of NSCDs at different time interval.
(A) UV−Vis absorption spectra after incubation of the NSCDs (from 0 to 25 min) into DPPH• solutions (0.02 mg/mL); (B) Scavenging activity curve as a function of the NSCDs concentration.
Percentage Scavenging activity of NSCDs towards (A) KMnO4; (B) Hydroxyl; (C) Superoxide; (D) Peroxide.
Percentage of total antioxidant capacity of NSCDs.

UV–Vis spectra of DPPH free radical in presence of NSCDs with different time interval at (A) pH=3; (B) pH=7; (C) pH=11; (D) EPR spectra of DPPH free radical in presence of NSCDs at three different pH (3,7,11).
Proposed mechanism for DPPH• free radical scavenging by NSCDs through HAT and PCET reactions.
Comparison of different analytical methods for the detection of Tartrazine.
Comparison of IC50 values of Free Radicals/Reactive Oxygen Species Reported through Different Scavenging Assay.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon dots for tartrazine sensing and mechanistic perception of their radical scavenging activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, West Bengal 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, West Bengal 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue fluorescent, biocompatible, nitrogen & sulphur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs), prepared via microwave-assisted pyrolysis of tartaric acid and thiourea, have been explored as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of the yellow-coloured food additive, tartrazine, in the aqueous medium through fluorescence quenching mechanism. The NSCDs (quantum yield ∼ 26%) exhibited static quenching in presence of tartrazine with the limit of detection (LOD) of 48 nM in the linear concentration range from 1 to 50 μM. In addition, NSCDs have also been investigated for their antioxidant activity by probing their ability to scavenge various reactive oxygen species (e.g., superoxide, peroxide, hydroxyl, and KMnO4) along with their total antioxidant capacity and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate (DPPH) free radicals premised on UV–Vis spectroscopy. From pH-dependent UV–Vis and EPR spectroscopy studies, the scavenging of DPPH free radicals by the NSCDs was affirmed to follow the hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) mechanism in an acidic medium while the proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) mechanism was predominant in alkaline medium.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon dots for tartrazine sensing and their radical scavenging activity

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanostructures, have intrigued the interest of researchers in recent years due to their potential application in various fields such as - catalysis [1], sensing [2], solar cells [3], and light-emitting devices [4], drug delivery [5], and bioimaging [6]. One of the most technologically relevant and fascinating properties of the CDs is their ability to exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission in the entire visible range, starting from the blue to the near-infrared region [7]. The origin of their unique fluorescence property have been attributed to the presence of graphitic core, surface emissive traps, and surface functional groups [8] thus, making them a promising fluorescent probe for the detection of various analytes such as toxic chemicals [9], biomolecule [10], metal ion [11] etc. Tartrazine, a widely used yellow-coloured food colourant, is one such chemical that necessitates selective and sensitive detection as their excessive consumption through food intake (FDA recommended permissible limit 7.5 mg/kg) can cause serious health problems such as; diarrhea, neurobehavioral poisonousness, and reproductive toxicity [12]. Although there are several reports on tartrazine sensing [13–15] using various chromogenic and fluorogenic probes however, CDs-based fluorescent probes are gaining popularity because of their good sensitivity, and selectivity as well as technical simplicity. Here, the visual readout of the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of CDs in presence of the target-analyte is exploited for ratiometric detection of the analyte. For instance, Hua Xu et al. recently showed that the CDs prepared from a green source, with fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of 10.37% (with respect to quinine sulfate), could serve as an effective sensor for sensitive and selective detection of tartrazine with a detection limit (LOD) of 73 nM in the linear concentration range 0.1–30 μM [12]. Similarly, Yang et al. also reported the hydrothermal preparation of N, Cl co-doped fluorescent CDs, with QY 60% (with respect to rhodamine 6 G) for selective and sensitive detection of tartrazine with LOD value of 68 nM in the linear range 0.1–30 µM [16]. Even though these studies have reported the use of CD-based fluorescent probes for tartrazine sensing but there is still scope to suitably functionalize the CD surfaces for achieving higher QY and lower LOD values.

Further, the CDs surface being endowed with functional groups such as -COOH and non-phenolic –OH can facilitate their ability to scavenge the reactive oxygen species (ROS/free radical) [17], which often get generated during any aerobic metabolic process in the living system in presence of molecular oxygen [18]. The scavenging ability of the CDs can provide a defense mechanism for reducing the oxidative stress in the cells that may arise due to the imbalance created between the rates of production and removal of ROS in the biological cells. Till date, studies reported on the radical scavenging properties of CDs have been limited to DPPH assay [19,20], which often makes the conclusions unconvincing therefore, it is imperative to include other assay processes to validate the findings.

In this study, we report the synthesis of nitrogen & sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) via one pot microwave assisted pyrolysis of tartaric acid and thiourea and have used them as a biocompatible fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of tartrazine by monitoring their fluorescence quenching. Doping CDs with non-metallic heteroatoms such as Nitrogen or Sulfur increases their electron density which is not only likely to aid in achieving higher quantum yield by the introduction of more defective sites to provide additional energy level [21], but also in enhancing their antioxidant activity. The quenching mechanism of the NSCDs in presence of tartrazine has been investigated through UV–Vis spectroscopy and lifetime decay studies using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC). Furthermore, attempts have been made to study the antioxidant property of NSCDs by investigating their radical scavenging ability, evaluated through different assays (i.e., DPPH assay, KMnO4 reduction assay, hydroxyl radical scavenging assay, superoxide radical anion scavenging assay, peroxide scavenging assay along with total antioxidant capacity) premised on UV–Vis spectroscopy studies. Subsequently, DPPH radical scavenging mechanism of the NSCDs have been validated through UV–Vis and EPR spectroscopy studies.

The XRD pattern of NSCDs (Fig. 1 A) was found to be analogous to graphitic carbon however, the interlayer spacing calculated for the d002 plane was found to be 0.38 nm compared to 0.34 nm for pure graphitic carbon. Moreover, the d002 line was found to be much broader and the corresponding 2θ value was shifted from ∼26˚ (in pure graphitic carbon) to ∼23.36˚. This can be attributed to poor graphitic crystallinity, and may be also due to hetero-atom doping and small average particle size [22].

The FTIR studies have been used to identify the functional groups present on NSCDs surface and is depicted in Fig. 1B. The wide band at 3198 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds, while the peak at 638 cm−1 indicates the out of plane bending of N–H bond. Sharp peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 770 cm−1 are attributed to C=O stretching vibrations and in plane bending of C–O bonds respectively. Two independent peaks at 1110 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of C–N or C–S bonds and C=S bond respectively, verifying the presence of N and S heteroatoms in the NSCD. Furthermore, the strong peak at 1351 cm−1 could be ascribed to the C–N bond stretching vibration, whilst the weak peak at 1650 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C=N and C=C group. The presence of COOH, OH, and NH2 functional groups on NSCDs can thus be inferred from FTIR studies which possibly contribute to their good solubility in aqueous medium [23].

The bright field TEM image of NSCDs depicted in Fig. 1C shows that the particles are spherical in shape and are well dispersed (Inset, Fig. 1C) with particle size ranging between 3 -7 nm and average diameter ∼ 4.7 nm. The d-spacing was calculated to be 0.375 nm that is well accorded with the XRD result. Therefore, the poor graphitic nature of NSCDs can also be seen from HRTEM image.

XPS study was used to investigate the surface states and elemental composition of the synthesized NSCDs. Fig. 2 A shows the XPS survey spectrum of NSCDs, in which five distinct peaks at 164 eV, 227 eV, 284.5 eV, 400 eV, and 531.2 eV were observed corresponding to S2p, S2s, C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively. From the high-resolution deconvulated XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2B), N1s (Fig. 2C), and S2p (Fig. 2D) [22], the relative atomic percentage of C, N and S are calculated to be 46.24, 16.47 and 7.91 respectively. Furthermore, the binding energy value at 287.6 eV in the deconvulated C1s (Fig. 2B) spectra may be due to the presence of carboxylic acid and amide linkage in the NSCDs, which probably got formed during the dehydration reaction of precursors. Similarly, the peaks at 399.7 and 400.2 eV in Fig. 2C the may respectively be assigned to pyridinic and pyrrolic N while peaks at 163.5 and 164.5 eV in Fig 2D can be respectively assigned to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 corresponding to the -C-S-C- bonds. The pyridinic, pyrrolic N and -C-S-C- moieties probably get generated during the carbonization process [24]. Thus, findings from XPS were in agreement with the FT-IR studies of NSCDs.

To study the optical properties of NSCDs, the UV−Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of NSCDs solution were recorded. The absorption spectrum of NSCD (Fig. 3 ) showed two broad absorption bands positioned around 250–270 nm and 320–350 nm. The absorption band at 250–270 nm can be attributed to the π→π* transition in the conjugated -C=C- units, which possibly constituted the carbon core, whereas the band at 320–350 nm may be assigned to the n→π* transition in C=O/C=N/C=S bonds [25,26] corresponding to the presence of carbonyl/amine functional groups on the NSCD surface.

The fluorescence emission studies of NSCDs reveals (Fig. 4 A) a red shift in the emission peak (λem) with gradual increase in the excitation wavelength (λ ex), with intensity occurring at λem = 450 nm corresponding to excitation wavelength of 360 nm falls in blue region. This excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission in NSCDs can probably be assigned to the n-π* transitions occurring in C=O/C–N/C–S that are present as functional groups conjugated to their surface [27]. The pH dependent fluorescence studies of the NSCDs in the range 3–11 (Fig. 4B) shows the emission spectra to remain unchanged in both acidic and neutral pH, which implies the predominance of acidic sites on the NSCD surface and possibly the reason for the blue emission [28].

The emission spectra of NSCDs were recorded at λex = 360 nm before and after the addition of tartrazine (Fig. 5 A). The fluorescence intensity of NSCDs was observed to quench progressively with successive additions of tartrazine, and the quenching efficiency could be fitted to the Stern–Volmer-equation [29]:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of NSCDs in the absence and presence of the quencher (i.e., tartrazine) at λem = 450 nm, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the quencher concentration (i.e., tartrazine). A linear correlation was observed between F0/F and the concentrations of tartrazine with a correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.998 and the Ksv value was found to be 7.65 × 104 M−1. The limit of detection (LOD) value was estimated to be 48 nM by using 3σ criterion (where, σ is the standard deviation of the graph at a signal to noise ratio of three) [30] in tartrazine concentration range of 1–50 μM (Fig. S2).

To evaluate the selectivity of tartrazine, the effect of intensity maxima (at λ em = 450 nm) of NSCDs in presence of different coexisting interfering agents (such as – K +, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, glucose, sucrose, lactose, vitamin C, and citric acid) were examined at λ ex = 360 nm under the same experimental conditions. The bar diagram showing F0/F (where, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of NSCDs in the absence and presence of tartrazine respectively) for the various interfering agents are depicted in Fig. 6 (A & B). It was observed that tartrazine could cause significant attenuation of fluorescence intensity of NSCDs compared to all the other coexisting interfering agents thus, making them potential as an efficient fluorescence probe for selective detection of tartrazine.

Comparison of the LOD values in the detection of tartrazine through various other methods is tabulated in Table 1 where, NSCDs were found to exhibit reasonably low limit of detection values when used as a fluorescent probe.

The fluorescence quenching of NSCDs in presence of tartrazine may occur due to various process such as inner filter effect, ground-state complex formation, excited state electron transfer (ET) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The absorbance of NSCDs solution have always been maintained below 0.1 to circumvent fluorescence quenching due to inner filter effect. The fluorescence quenching is known to follow the static quenching mechanism (SQ) if the probe (i.e., NSCDs) forms ground state complex with the quencher (i.e., tartrazine), while the excited state ET and FRET phenomenon are associated with dynamic quenching (DQ) [35]. Linearity of the Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 5B) can only ascertain that the fluorescence quenching may have occurred either through SQ or DQ. Therefore, the change in UV–Vis absorption spectra and measurement of lifetime decay of the probe (NSCDs) through time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) have been carried out to understand the quenching mechanism followed in this system. From the UV–Vis spectra, the absorption maxima of tartrazine (Fig. 5C) were found to be shifted with the addition of NSCDs probably due to the formation of ground-state complex of tartrazine-NSCDs. From TCSPC (Table-S1) results, it was observed that fluorescence lifetimes of NSCDs showed no significant change in the absence (τ0) and presence (τ) of various concentrations of tartrazine, which confirmed the quenching mechanism to be static. In Table 1, we compare the experimental results with those for reported methods of tartrazine detection.

The unpaired electron present on N atom of DPPH molecule gives a strong EPR signal with g-factor of 2.0036, which is typical g-value for free electron. After ten minute, no changes in DPPH signal were observed, signifying the stability of free radical in ethanoic solution. The ethanoic solution of NSCDs (100μL) were then added with DPPH (1 mM) free radical and monitored the DPPH signal got diminished and reached the lowest amplitude in less than 10 min of addition, indicating the potential free radical scavenging ability of NSCDs (Fig. 7). The EPR results motivate to explore the scavenging property of NSCDs through several other in-vitro assay such as, KMnO4 assay, hydroxyl free radical assay, superoxide radical anion assay and peroxide anion assay.

From the study of DPPH reduction assay, time dependent UV–Vis spectra were recorded (depicted in Fig. 8A), at λ max=517 nm, where the absorbance started decreasing immediately after the addition of a fixed concentration of NSCDs (10 µl from 1 mg/ml stock) and after 30 min, more than 70% DPPH free radical concentrations were neutralized. after the scavenging activity of the solution increases as the concentration of NSCDs increased from 0.02 to 0.10 mg/ml (Fig. 8B). The DPPH free radical scavenging activities of NSCDs were found to be 18.2%, 39.5%, 70.0%, 88.4%, and 93.2% (averaged value based on three trials) with the concentrations of 0.02, 0.032, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.103 mg/mL, respectively. Beyond the concentration of NSCDs >0.075 mg/mL, the scavenging activity become constant and reaches a plateau. Therefore, the IC50 value of NSCDs was found to be very low (0.04 mg mL−1) in comparison to ascorbic acid (0.2 mg mL−1).

This reaction occurs in an acidic medium, where a redox reaction (Mn (VII) to Mn (II) or Mn (IV)) between the scavenger and potassium permanganate leads to a change in color until disappearance. The degradation of the KMnO4 solution has been monitored by observing the change in color which has a direct correlation with the increase in the concentration of the NSCDs. The reduction of KMnO4 was found to increase with the increase sin concentration of NSCD (0.1–0.8 mg ml−1) as observed in Fig. 9A, ascertained that the NSCDs is found to have a strong reducing capacity. NSCDs scavenging properties reached the IC50 value around 0.09 mg mL−1, which is slightly higher than the standard scavenger L-ascorbic acid's IC50 value (0.075 mg mL−1).

Hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of NSCDs has been demonstrated through Fenton reaction, where degradation of salicylic acid by hydroxyl radical (•OH) used as a probe. Here salicylic acid has certain advantage to use as a probe molecule for detecting •OH, as the second-order rate constant of the reaction between salicylic acid and •OH is high enough (k is approximately 5 × 10−9 M−1S−1) to enable competitive kinetics between the probe and the tested scavenger. The generation of •OH is detected by its ability to degrade salicylic acid to form products (2,3-dihydroxy benzoic acid (2,3-DHBA); 2,5-dihydroxy benzoic acid (2,5-DHBA); and catechol (CT)). Fig. 9B shows that the hydroxyl radical activity decreases as the concentration of NSCDs rises (0.1–0.8 mg ml−1), inhibiting the breakdown of salicylic acid. The hydroxyl radical scavenging properties of NSCDs are similar to those of the conventional scavenger molecule (L-ascorbic acid) with IC50 value 0.27 mg ml−1.

The scavenging of superoxide anion free radical by NSCDs was demonstrated using the pyrogallol molecule as a probe. When pyrogallol is combined with buffer (pH=8.2), it undergoes autoxidation, resulting in the creation of superoxide radical, reacts further to form quinone derivative, showing a broad UV–Vis spectrum with absorbance at λmax =325 nm. After the addition of different concentration of NSCDs (10–100 μg mL−1), superoxide get scavenged, leading to the inhibition of quinone derivative formation, resulting in decreased the absorbance at 325 nm. At the NSCDs concentration increased to 40 mg/ml, more than 50% superoxide were neutralized (Fig. 9C).

The ability of NSCDs to scavenge hydrogen peroxide(H2O2) has been demonstrated and compared with that of ascorbic acid as reference compound. With increasing concentration of NSCDs, the UV–Vis absorption at λmax =230 nm was found to decrease indicating the gradual oxidation of hydrogen peroxide to molecular oxygen. The IC50 value for NSCDs was obtained around 66 μg/mL (depicted in Fig. 9D) with the final scavenging activity was estimated to be 60%. (IC50 value for standard scavenger molecule, L-Ascorbic acid, was 10.14 μg/mL).

Phosphomolybdate assay is a quantitative method to estimate the total antioxidant capacity (TAC) of antioxidants [36] Various NSCDs concentrations (0–70 μg/mL) were added to phosphomolybdate reagent to estimate TAC, depicted in Fig. 10, showing a characteristic increase in the absorption at wavelength 695 nm due to phosphomolybdate(V) complex formation. With the increase in the concentration of NSCDs (0–70 μg/mL), TAC was found to increase from 30% and reached a maximum of 85% at 70 μg/mL concentration of NSCDs. The formation of phosphomolybdate (V) complex can be explained as the result of reduction of Molybdenum (VI to V) due to the presence of electron-donating groups on the surface of NSCDs (like -NH2). The IC50 was calculated to be 37 μg/mL (IC50 value for standard scavenger molecule, L-Ascorbic acid, was 0.65 μg/mL). A comparison of IC50 for various ROS has been summarized below (Table 2).

DPPH• has been reported to be quenched by hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), single electron transfer (SET), or proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) [37]. To unveil the free radical scavenging mechanism of NSCDs, the UV–Vis spectra of DPPH radical in presence of NSCDs were studied at different pH values (pH = 3, 7, 11), as is shown in Fig. 11A, B, C. The absorbance maxima of DPPH were diminished by 70%, 50%, and 90% at pH values of 3, 7, and 11 respectively. Thus, it is evident that the scavenging activity was substantially enhanced in both acidic and basic solutions, compared to a neutral solution (pH=7). This observation was corroborated with the pH-dependent EPR spectra, where the peak intensity of the DPPH radical was found to follow the same trend (Fig. 11D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism of scavenging activity by NSCD
ce:section.ce:para: From the pH-dependent UV–Vis and EPR studies at pH values of 3, 7, and 11, it can be hypothesized that de/protonation of the carboxyl (–COOH) and the amino (–NH2) functional groups on the NSCDs surface stimulated their DPPH• radical scavenging activity either through direct or through indirect HAT mechanism (Scheme I). From attenuation of the DPPH• signal in UV–Vis and EPR spectra, it could be inferred that at low pH (pH ≤ 3), protonated amine groups on NSCD surface could convert the DPPH• radical to DPPH–H complex by donating a H atom [38]. Thus, high concentration of H + together with abundance of acidic functional groups on the NSCDs surface possibly favoured the DPPH• radical scavenging through HAT process. In comparison, in the basic medium the DPPH• radical experienced the highest attenuation of signal, probably due to the electron-donating ability of surface –NH2 group present on the NSCD surface. This facilitated the transfer of electron from the amine group to DPPH• radical via formation of aminium radical cation (NSCD–NH2•+) and DPPH ion (DPPH–). The aminium radical cation (NSCD–NH2•+) eventually undergo deprotonation to generate the DPPH–H complex thus, entailing the prevalence of the PCET process in the scavenging mechanism in the basic medium. Further, both electron transfer and deprotonation is favoured by the polarity of the ethanol solution, which provides the necessary solvation energy to stabilize the ionic species and hence facilitates higher rates of the PCET process compared to the HAT process. The possibility of the occurrence of the SET process in the DPPH• radical scavenging mechanism may be precluded in absence of any additional free radical signal in pH-dependent EPR spectra. It could thus be inferred that the DPPH• free radical scavenging occurs through the HAT process in acidic medium while it is through the PCET process in basic medium although, the possibility of co-occurrence of HAT process in the basic medium is difficult to rule out. It can therefore be reasoned that the functional groups (i.e., –COOH and –NH2) on NSCDs surface play a key role in ascertaining their DPPH• free radical scavenging activity. Since DPPH• free radical is analogous to other ROS consequently, their scavenging by NSCDs may also be presumed to follow the same HAT or PCET process.

Blue emissive, N and S co-doped CDs (NSCDs) with a quantum yield 26% were synthesized through rapid, one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis of thiourea and tartaric acid. They have been favourably used as a fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive detection of tartrazine in the broad linear concentration range of 1 – 50 µM with LOD value of 48 nM. Fluorescence life time measurement studies ascertained the fluorescence quenching of NSCDs, in presence of Tartrazine, followed the static quenching mechanism, possibly due to the formation of ground-state tartrazine – NSCDs complex. Further, pH-dependent UV–Vis and EPR studies premised on DPPH• radical revealed that the amine and carboxyl groups present on the NSCD surface facilitated their radical scavenging ability in alkaline and acidic pH via (proton coupled electron transfer) PCET and (hydrogen atom transfer) HAT mechanism respectively. Low IC50 values for ROS/radical scavenging along with low cytotoxicity of NSCDs validated their potential as antioxidants.
Md Abdus Salam Shaik- Conceptualization, Experimentation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing, Editing – original draft preparation.
Dipanjan Samanta- Investigation, Reviewing and Editing
Manisha Shaw- Investigation, Reviewing and Editing
Imran Mondal- Reviewing and Editing
Rajarshi Basu- Reviewing and Editing

1. Preparation of NSCD, 2. Characterization Techniques of NSCDs, 3. Quantum yield Calculation (Fig. S1), Spectra of NSCDs quenching in full range and its corresponding Stern-Volmer plot from 1–50 µM (Fig. S2), Methods of Sensing, Methods of different assay, TCSPC data (Table S1), Cytotoxicity Study (Fig. S3)

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Central Research facility and the Department of Chemistry, IIT Kharagpur India. Md A S Shaik would also like to acknowledge IIT Kharagpur, India for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.snr.2022.100127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots/SiO2 porous nanocomposites and their catalytic ability for photo-enhanced hydrocarbon selective oxidation
Dalt. Trans.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Han et al., “Synthesis of carbon quantum dots/SiO2 porous nanocomposites and their catalytic ability for photo-enhanced hydrocarbon selective oxidation,” Dalt. Trans., vol. 42, no. 29, pp. 10380–10383, 2013, doi: 10.1039/C3DT51165K.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for ‘off-on’ sensing of Hg(II) and I−
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. He, et al., Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for .. off-on... sensing of Hg(II) and I..., Biosens. Bioelectron. 79 (2016) 531...535, doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.12.084.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Förster resonance energy transfer and carbon dots enhance light harvesting in a solid-state quantum dot solar cell
J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Narayanan, M. Deepa, and A.K. Srivastava, “Förster resonance energy transfer and carbon dots enhance light harvesting in a solid-state quantum dot solar cell,” J. Mater. Chem. A, vol. 1, no. 12, pp. 3907–3918, 2013, doi: 10.1039/C3TA01601C.
